paper.title_en: Quench Characteristics and Protection of the CEPC Detector Magnet
paper.abstract_en: The circular electron positron collider (CEPC) detector magnet is a large-scale superconducting magnet with stringent quench protection requirements. To enhance the mechanical stability of the coils, the aluminum-stabilized NbTi/Cu cable adopts a box-type structure, in which part of the outer high-purity aluminum is replaced by high-strength aluminum alloy. This modification leads to a degradation in the quench characteristics and thermal stability of the coils. This article comprehensively evaluates a protection strategy combining a 50 mΩ dump resistor with the quench-back effect provided by the support cylinder. Rapid assessment using the MIITS (integral of I<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> over time) method confirmed that this configuration maintains the terminal voltage below 1 kV and limits the maximum coil temperature to 59.22 K. A detailed 3-D finite element analysis further quantified the quench behavior: the peak hotspot temperature reached 56.7 K, and the current decay time constant is 140 s, underscoring the contribution of the quench-back cylinder. The minimum quench energy is calculated to range from 16.4 J to 20 J, depending on the quench initiation location. The magnet’s intrinsic self-protecting capability is demonstrated under various fault conditions; peak temperatures remained within acceptable limits even with protection delays up to 10 s, and reached a maximum of 97.42 K only in the event of a complete protection system failure. These results validate the reliability of the quench protection design for the CEPC detector magnet.
